---
title: "Convolutions and Theorems"
---

::: {.hidden}
 \providecommand{\vv}[1]{{\mathbf{#1}}}
 \providecommand{\pd}[2]{{\frac{\partial #1}{\partial #2}}}
:::

## Parseval's theorem
Recall Parseval's theorem for Fourier series in complex exponential form:
$$
\frac{1}{L} \int_0^L |f(x)|^2\,dx = \sum_{n=-\infty}^\infty |c_n|^2.
$$

Thinking of Fourier transforms as a continuous version of this discrete sum, we get Parseval's theorem for Fourier transforms:
$$
\int_{-\infty}^\infty |f(x)|^2\,dx= \int_{-\infty}^\infty |\hat f(k)|^2\,dk
$$
The proof is straightforward.

## Convolution theorem
### What is a convolution?
If $f(x)$ and $g(x)$ are two functions, their convolution is a new function written
$$
(f*g)(x).
$$
It is defined by
$$
(f*g)(x)=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(\xi)\,g(x-\xi)\,d\xi.
$$

You can think of this as:
1. Slide one function across the other.
2. Multiply them where they overlap.
3. Add up (integrate) the result.

So convolution is a way of mixing two signals/functions.

### Example (convolution of functions)
Let
$$
f(x)=g(x)=
\begin{cases}
1 & |x|\le \frac{1}{2},\\
0 & |x|>\frac{1}{2}.
\end{cases}
$$
Find $(f*g)(x)$.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Solution
By definition,
$$
(f*g)(x)=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(\xi)\,g(x-\xi)\,d\xi.
$$
Here, $f(\xi)=1$ only on $\left[-\frac12,\frac12\right]$ and $g(x-\xi)=1$ only on $\left[x-\frac12,x+\frac12\right]$.
So the integral is just the overlap length of these two intervals.

If $|x|>1$, there is no overlap, so $(f*g)(x)=0$.

If $|x|\le1$, the overlap length is $1-|x|$, so $(f*g)(x)=1-|x|$.

Therefore
$$
(f*g)(x)=
\begin{cases}
1-|x| & |x|\le 1,\\
0 & |x|>1.
\end{cases}
$$
So convolving two box pulses gives a triangle pulse.
:::

### Convolution theorem (Fourier transforms)
With our Fourier transform convention, the key result is:
$$
\widehat{f*g}(k)=\sqrt{2\pi}\,\hat f(k)\,\hat g(k).
$$

So:
1. Convolution in $x$-space becomes multiplication in $k$-space, up to the factor $\sqrt{2\pi}$.
2. Multiplication in $x$-space becomes convolution in $k$-space, up to the factor $1/\sqrt{2\pi}$.

Equivalently,
$$
\widehat{f\,g}(k)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}(\hat f * \hat g)(k).
$$

### Example (using the theorem, same functions)
Using the same box function
$$
f(x)=g(x)=
\begin{cases}
1 & |x|\le \frac{1}{2},\\
0 & |x|>\frac{1}{2},
\end{cases}
$$
we have
$$
\hat f(k)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-1/2}^{1/2}e^{-ikx}\,dx
=\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}}\frac{\sin(k/2)}{k}.
$$
At $k=0$, this is understood by continuity, giving $\hat f(0)=1/\sqrt{2\pi}$.

Since $f=g$, the convolution theorem gives
$$
\widehat{f*g}(k)=\sqrt{2\pi}\,\hat f(k)\hat g(k)
=\sqrt{2\pi}\,\big(\hat f(k)\big)^2
=2\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}}\left(\frac{\sin(k/2)}{k}\right)^2.
$$
So in Fourier space, the convolution becomes a product up to a constant factor.

::: {.callout-tip}
For calculations, this is very useful: convolutions are hard, but multiplication is easy.
:::
